---
title: "04 Trees"
resources:
  - trees_project.ipynb
  - adult_lightgbm.ipynb
  - data/titanic.csv
---

::: {.callout-note}
рЯУЭ ’Н’ђ’°’µ’§’•÷А’® ’Ї’°’њ÷А’°’љ’њ ’•’ґ (’ґ’•÷А÷Д÷З’Є÷В’і)÷Й ‘і’°’љ’•÷А’® ’§’•’Љ ’±’°’µ’ґ’°’£÷А’Њ’°’Ѓ ’є’•’ґ вАФ ’њ’•’љ’°’ґ’µ’Є÷В’©’•÷А’® ’ѓ’°’Њ’•’ђ’°÷Б’Њ’•’ґ ’§’°’љ’•÷А’Ђ÷Б ’∞’•’њ’Є÷Й
:::

# рЯО≤ Random

TBD

# рЯУЪ ’Ж’µ’Є÷В’©’®

’Н’ђ’°’µ’§’•÷А’® `ml/04_trees/` ’Ї’°’ґ’°’ѓ’Є÷В’івА§

- **[17] Decision trees** вАФ anatomy of a tree, Gini / entropy + information gain, the CART algorithm, greedy (not optimal) splits, overfitting вЖТ pruning (`ccp_alpha`), axis-aligned staircases, and why trees cannot extrapolate. [PDF](https://github.com/HaykTarkhanyan/python_math_ml_course/blob/main/ml/04_trees/17_decision_trees.pdf)
- **[18] Random forests** вАФ bootstrap aggregating, the variance formula (the $\rho\sigma^2$ floor), OOB score, `max_features` as the decorrelation knob, importances, and smoother boundaries than one tree. [PDF](https://github.com/HaykTarkhanyan/python_math_ml_course/blob/main/ml/04_trees/18_random_forests.pdf)
- **[19] Boosting** вАФ AdaBoost, gradient boosting as gradient descent in function space, the residual animation, learning rate + early stopping, and classification in log-odds. [PDF](https://github.com/HaykTarkhanyan/python_math_ml_course/blob/main/ml/04_trees/19_boosting.pdf)
- **[20] Advanced boosting** вАФ XGBoost (regularized objective вЖТ structure score), LightGBM (leaf-wise, GOSS, EFB), CatBoost (ordered target statistics), monotonic constraints, and stacking. [PDF](https://github.com/HaykTarkhanyan/python_math_ml_course/blob/main/ml/04_trees/20_advanced_boosting.pdf)

рЯУЭ **‘є’•’і’°’µ’Ђ ’Њ’•÷А’°’Ґ’•÷А’µ’°’ђ ’∞’°÷А÷Б’°’Ј’°÷А (Google Form):** TBD

# рЯП° ’П’ґ’°’µ’Ђ’ґ

## Chapter project вАФ the Titanic running score рЯІАрЯІА

Build one classifier **three ways** and keep a **running scoreboard** on the same Titanic train/test split: a single tree (HW1) вЖТ a random forest (HW2) вЖТ gradient boosting (HW3). Report accuracy + F1 / ROC-AUC at each step and watch how - and *whether* - each ensemble beats the last. (The lectures do this too, and the answer on this small, one-dominant-feature dataset is not the textbook one.)

Starter notebook: <a href="trees_project.ipynb" download>trees_project.ipynb (download)</a> ¬Ј [view on GitHub](https://github.com/HaykTarkhanyan/python_math_ml_course/blob/main/ml/04_trees/trees_project.ipynb). Data: `data/titanic.csv` (pinned from [OpenML](https://www.openml.org/d/40945), so no live fetch needed).

### HW1 вАФ grow, prune, read a tree ([17])

1. **By hand** (Play Tennis): build the decision stump on Outlook and compute the information gain of *one* other split (Humidity or Wind).
2. **sklearn** (Titanic): fit a `DecisionTreeClassifier`; plot the depth train/test **U-curve**.
3. **Prune** via `ccp_alpha` chosen by cross-validation; visualize the final tree (`plot_tree`).
4. Confirm **scale-invariance**: rescale a feature, refit, show the tree is unchanged.

*Bonus:* feature importances + compare to your chapter-3 logistic-regression baseline; refit the single tree as `LGBMRegressor(n_estimators=1, learning_rate=1.0)` and check it matches. Further reading: the r2d3 visual intro to machine learning.

### HW2 вАФ bag the tree ([18])

1. Fit a `RandomForestClassifier`; report the **OOB score** vs 5-fold **CV**; compare **accuracy** to your HW1 single tree (the running score), and also report **F1 / ROC-AUC** (Titanic is imbalanced).
2. Sweep `n_estimators` and plot the **plateau**; sweep `max_features` and watch it matter.
3. Read `feature_importances_`; note any high-cardinality bias.
4. **Regression:** `RandomForestRegressor` on the Yerevan rent toy; plot error vs `n_estimators`.

*Bonus:* swap in `ExtraTreesClassifier` and compare; argue from the variance formula why averaging *cannot* beat the $\rho\sigma^2$ floor.

### HW3 вАФ boost the tree ([19]вАУ[20])

1. **By hand** (Yerevan rent toy): do **3 rounds** of gradient boosting вАФ init $= \text{mean}(y)$, compute residuals, fit a stump, update with $\eta$. Show the residuals shrinking.
2. **sklearn** (Titanic): fit a `GradientBoostingClassifier` / `HistGradientBoostingClassifier` with early stopping; compare to your HW2 random forest on the same score (accuracy + F1 / ROC-AUC).
3. Tune `learning_rate` (with early stopping picking `n_estimators`). Then try **LightGBM** and add a **monotonic constraint** on a feature whose effect you expect to be monotone.

*Bonus:* the AdaBoost $\alpha = \tfrac12\ln\frac{1-\text{err}}{\text{err}}$ arithmetic for one round; force overfitting (train $\to$ 0, test U-turn) then early-stop; try `subsample` $< 1$.

## Practical вАФ LightGBM on Census Income рЯІАрЯІАрЯІА

A **minimal-scaffold** capstone: use **LightGBM as one lens** on the whole chapter вАФ the *same* library gives you a single tree, a random forest, and gradient boosting, so the only thing that changes between them is the ensembling strategy (not the implementation, the preprocessing, or the metric). Dataset: UCI **Adult / Census Income** (~48,800 people; predict income `>$50K`), which is **imbalanced** (~24% positive) with **categorical** features and **missing values**. Because ~76% earn `<=50K`, **report ROC-AUC and F1, not accuracy.**

Starter notebook (loader + task list only вАФ *you* do the cleaning and all the modelling): <a href="adult_lightgbm.ipynb" download>adult_lightgbm.ipynb (download)</a> ¬Ј [view on GitHub](https://github.com/HaykTarkhanyan/python_math_ml_course/blob/main/ml/04_trees/adult_lightgbm.ipynb). Data loads live via `fetch_openml("adult", version=2)` ([UCI Adult](https://archive.ics.uci.edu/dataset/2/adult)).

- **Part 0 вАФ clean & explore:** binary target + imbalance; missing values (LightGBM handles `NaN` natively, no imputation needed); drop `fnlwgt` (a census *sampling weight*) and the duplicated `education` / `education-num`; mark the 8 categoricals (incl. `native-country`, 41 levels); one **stratified** split reused everywhere.
- **Part 1 вАФ three baselines, one library:** single tree (`n_estimators=1, learning_rate=1`), random forest (`boosting_type='rf'`), gradient boosting (default `gbdt`) вАФ scored on the same test split (**ROC-AUC + F1**). Then **native `categorical_feature` vs one-hot** (watch `native-country` blow up the column count). Expect tree < RF вЙ≤ GBDT.
- **Part 2 вАФ make the GBDT win:** early stopping; tune in the [20] order (`learning_rate` вЖФ trees вЖТ `num_leaves` / `min_data_in_leaf` вЖТ `lambda_l1/l2` вЖТ subsampling); handle imbalance (`is_unbalance` / `scale_pos_weight`) and pick the decision threshold by F1.
- **Part 3 вАФ read it:** gain vs **permutation** importance; optional **monotonic constraint** on `education-num`; a short reflection on whether tree < RF < GBDT actually held, and by how much.

*Bonus:* add **XGBoost** and compare; build a **stacking** ensemble (LightGBM + logistic regression) with out-of-fold predictions.


<a href="http://s01.flagcounter.com/more/1oO"><img src="https://s01.flagcounter.com/count2/1oO/bg_FFFFFF/txt_000000/border_CCCCCC/columns_2/maxflags_10/viewers_0/labels_0/pageviews_1/flags_0/percent_0/" alt="Flag Counter"></a>
